relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: Israel falls again, and Midian's hand closes over the land.
Oppression drives people into caves, dens, and strongholds.
Every sowing becomes an invitation for raiders.
Food, animals, and sustenance are stripped away.
The invaders arrive like locusts, too many to count.
Israel is brought very low and finally cries out.
The cry rises because of Midian.
A prophet answers with Exodus memory.
Liberation from Egypt and oppressors is remembered again.
The rebuke names fear of local gods and refusal to listen.
Gideon hides wheat in a winepress while the messenger sits beneath the terebinth.
The hidden survivor is addressed as mighty.
Gideon dares to ask why abandonment feels stronger than wonder.
The answer sends him in the strength that includes his protest.
Gideon names his smallness and weak position.
Presence, not status, becomes the promise.
He asks for a sign that the voice is truly speaking.
The messenger waits while Gideon prepares an offering.
Meat, broth, and unleavened cakes become the material of encounter.
The food is placed upon rock and poured out.
Fire rises from rock, and the messenger disappears.
Gideon realizes whom he has seen and fears death.
Peace answers fear: you shall not die.
Gideon names the altar YHWH-shalom before any battle is won.
That night he is told to tear down his father's Baal altar and Asherah.
A new altar is built from the dismantled materials of the old.
Gideon obeys at night because fear still lives in him.
Morning exposes the broken altar and new offering.
The town investigates until Gideon is named.
The men demand his death for the broken altar.
Joash turns the mob back by asking whether Baal needs human defense.
Gideon receives the name Jerubbaal: let Baal contend.
The eastern armies gather in Jezreel.
Spirit clothes Gideon, and the shofar calls his clan.
Messengers gather Manasseh, Asher, Zebulun, and Naphtali.
Gideon still asks whether this rescue is truly by his hand.
He places fleece on the threshing floor for a sign.
The fleece holds enough dew to fill a bowl.
Still afraid, he asks for the sign reversed.
The fleece alone stays dry while dew covers the ground.

contemplative_note: Judges 6 is not a macho origin story. Gideon is afraid, hiding food, questioning abandonment, obeying at night, and asking twice for confirmation. The holy thread is not fearlessness; it is that peace speaks first, then courage grows inside fear.
